coredata.dc:title: Effect of formulation and process on morphology and electrical conductivity of Ag-graphene hybrid inks
coredata.dc:creator: Ghadimi, Saeedeh
Mazinani, Saeedeh
Bazargan, Ali Mohammad
Sharif, Farhad
coredata.dc:description: Conductive inks are considered very promising for device fabrication on flexible substrates and large areas. Silver (Ag)-graphene hybrid inks offer many opportunities, including cost/conductivity optimization for different applications. In this work, several conductive hybrid ink formulations containing Ag nanostructures and graphene derivatives were prepared using different processing strategies and thoroughly characterized. SEM and morphological studies were used to explore the probable reasons for the difference in electrical conductivity of various inks. Strong interaction between Ag nanoparticles and graphene layers prevented the formation of island structures in films, especially at high temperatures. While Ag nanowires and graphene layers formed a uniform, dense three-dimensional network, offering high electrical conductivity. Additionally, an improvement of electrical conductivity by using graphene quantum dots as a graphene component in hybrid inks was observed, which was related to creating new conductive bridges. The highest conductivity of prepared inks varied in the range of 2.67 × 102 S/cm to 4.0 × 104 S/cm. Finally, formulation guidelines were offered to be used for inks by several printing methods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hybrid conductive ink
Graphene oxide
Graphene quantum dots
Silver nanoparticles
Inkjet printing
Thermal sintering

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of AgNPs, AgNPs/rGO and AgNWs/rGO inks
Preparation of Ag and AgGQDs inks
Effect of sintering temperature on electrical conductivity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Electrical resistivity as a function of sintering temperature of (a) AgNPs film, (b) Ag/rGO1, and (c) Ag/rGO2 nanocomposite films, which were prepared by two different methods, and (d-f) Ag/rGO nanocomposite films in different ratios of AgNO3: GO (d) 8, (e) 10, and (f) 20, Comparison of the electrical performance of two methods in each ratio as well as similar trends can be obsereved.
Electrical resistivity of (a) AgNWs/rGO-10 film as a function of temperature for 15 min (b) Ag film at different temperatures for 30 min, and (b) AgGQDs films at 100 °C for 30 min.

(a) XRD pattern with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.1542 nm) of GO, AgNPs, Ag/rGO1–10, and Ag/rGO2–10 powders, indicating the formation of AgNPs in the presence of GO. (b) Raman spectra of GO, Ag/rGO1–10, and Ag/rGO2–10 films with 532 nm excitation wavelength, confirming the charge transfers between the AgNPs and GO and decrease of functional groups on GO.

FESEM images of Ag-graphene nanocomposites prepared by the first method (a) Ag/rGO1–8, (b) Ag/rGO1–10, and (c) Ag/GO1–20 and prepared by the second method (d) Ag/rGO2–8, (e) Ag/rGO2–10, and (f) Ag/rGO2–20, confirming the attachment of AgNPs on graphene sheets and change in nanoparticle size and distribution as a function of the AgNO3: GO mass ratio.
(a) XRD pattern with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.1542 nm) of AgNWs and AgNWs/rGO-10, and (b) SEM images of AgNWsGr-10 nanocomposite at different magnifications.

(a) Schematic illustration showing the effect of reducing the amount of silver precursor and the change in the percentage of GQDs on the film formation process of Ag and AgGQDs inks, and FESEM images of (b) Ag film, (c) AgGQDs0 film, and (d) AgGQDs1 film after sintering at 100 °C for 30 min.

FESEM images of AgNPs films at two different magnifications after sintering for 15 min at (a, b) 150 °C, (c, d) 250 °C, and (e, f) 350 °C, The yellow and blue lines mark conductive paths and discontinuous regions, respectively.
FESEM images of Ag/rGO2–8 and Ag/rGO2–20 films at different magnifications after sintering for 15 min at (a, c) 200 °C and (b, d) 400 °C.

SEM images of AgNWs/rGO-10 films at different magnifications after sintering for 15 min at (a) 100 °C, (b) 170 °C, (c) 300 °C and (d) 400 °C, demonstrating improvement of the intertwined structure of AgNWs and rGO sheets with temperature increase and its degradation at high temperatures.
FESEM images of Ag films after heat treatment for 30 min at temperatures of (a) 60 °C and (b) 100 °C, indicating a dense structure after sintering at 100 °C.
Viscosity curves as a function of shear rate of (a) Ag/rGO1–10 ink, (b) AgGQDs1 ink, and (c) AgNWs/rGO-10 inks using HEC and CMC as viscosity modifiers. All measurements were taken at 25 °C.
Specifications of prepared Ag and AgGQDs inks.
The lowest electrical resistivity and the highest electrical conductivity of inks after sintering for 15 min.

head.ce:title: Effect of formulation and process on morphology and electrical conductivity of Ag-graphene hybrid inks

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer Engineering & Color Technology, Amirkabir University of Technology, P.O. Box 15875-4413, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer Engineering & Color Technology, Amirkabir University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Polymer Engineering & Color Technology, Amirkabir University of Technology, P.O. Box 15875–4413, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: New Technologies Research Center (NTRC), Amirkabir University of Technology, 15875-4413 Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: New Technologies Research Center (NTRC), Amirkabir University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: New Technologies Research Center (NTRC), Amirkabir University of Technology, 15875–4413, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Conductive inks are considered very promising for device fabrication on flexible substrates and large areas. Silver (Ag)-graphene hybrid inks offer many opportunities, including cost/conductivity optimization for different applications. In this work, several conductive hybrid ink formulations containing Ag nanostructures and graphene derivatives were prepared using different processing strategies and thoroughly characterized. SEM and morphological studies were used to explore the probable reasons for the difference in electrical conductivity of various inks. Strong interaction between Ag nanoparticles and graphene layers prevented the formation of island structures in films, especially at high temperatures. While Ag nanowires and graphene layers formed a uniform, dense three-dimensional network, offering high electrical conductivity. Additionally, an improvement of electrical conductivity by using graphene quantum dots as a graphene component in hybrid inks was observed, which was related to creating new conductive bridges. The highest conductivity of prepared inks varied in the range of 2.67 × 102 S/cm to 4.0 × 104 S/cm. Finally, formulation guidelines were offered to be used for inks by several printing methods.

ce:section.ce:para: The printing of electronic devices on flexible and rigid substrates as an emerging technology is developing rapidly [1–3]. This technology is applied in various fields such as health, advertising, automotive, transportation, energy, and electronics. Thus, printed electronics will play a significant role in electronic systems in the upcoming decades [1–6].

One of the main elements of printed electronics technology is the printing ink, where conductive inks are the most widely used. Many types of conductive inks with various fillers have been developed for printed electronics, including conductive polymers, graphene, carbon nanotubes, metal nanoparticles, metal nanowires, or organic metal [7–9]. Due to the poor conductivity, low thermal and chemical stability, the usage of polymer-based ink is limited. Silver-based ink is extensively utilized among traditional metal-based inks such as Au, Pt, Ag, and Cu inks, owing to its good electrical conductivity, high stability, cost-effectiveness, and anti-oxidant characteristics [10,11]. There are several kinds of silver-based inks; those are based on silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) [12,13], silver nanowires (AgNWs) [7,14], and silver-organic complex [15,16] inks, with different applications and properties.

In current years, the combination of metal nanoparticles and graphene nanosheets has been studied. Graphene is a carbon-based conductive material that has attracted much attention due to its unique chemical-physical structure with excellent electrical, thermal, and mechanical properties [17–19]. Therefore, it is a promising candidate as a silver complementary for conductive inks [1,20]. Consequently, these two materials’ full benefits can be used to increase graphene nanosheets conductivity and diminish the concentration of metal particles [21].

Graphene oxide (GO) and reduced GO (rGO) have been used in much research in Ag-graphene hybrid conductive inks [11,22–26]. Different types of hybrid inks have been developed using AgNPs as fillers or Ag-organic complexes. The difference in the silver base can differ in the inks preparation process, formulation, and final properties. In brief, Zhang et al. synthesized an Ag/rGO composite with sufficient conductivity and dispersion as a suitable ink filler for inkjet printing technology. They controlled Ag/rGO composites conductivity by changing the reaction time for anchoring AgNPs on the surface of rGO sheets. They demonstrated that AgNPs as spacers prevent the aggregation of rGO sheets and, consequently, nozzle clogging. The electrical conductivity of the prepared patterns was 2.0 × 103 S/m [11]. Wang et al. prepared a hybrid conductive ink using AgNPs and graphene. By investigating the effect of sintering temperature, time, pressure, and different percentages of graphene on electrical and mechanical performance of sintered electrical paths, they finally reached the optimal electrical resistivity of 6.19 × 10−8 Ω.m [19]. Annealed graphene nanosheets were studied by Wang et al. with an organic-Ag complex in a mixture of terpineol and cyclohexanone. AgNPs were uniformly distributed in graphene nanosheets after the annealing process, which could act as a conductive bridge to decrease the contact resistance of graphene flakes in printed paths. Graphene nanosheets interwoven with silver nanoparticles in printed films formed a three-dimensional conductive network with good mechanical strength. The conductivity of 2.16 × 103 S/m after 15 printing times and sintering at 300 °C for 40 min was achieved [27]. Yang et al. prepared the Ag/rGO hybrid ink consisting of Ag-organic complex and rGO via a one-step method. They showed that Ag/rGO films had better electrical properties than Ag films at low sintering temperatures (150–200 °C). They concluded that improving Ag/rGO films conductivity is due to rGO sheets, facilitating electron transfer between AgNPs [1]. In previous studies about AgNWs/rGO ink preparation, AgNWs ink was coated on the substrate surface. After solvent evaporation, GO solution formed a film on the surface of the as-prepared AgNWs substrate [28–31]. Therefore, it could not be considered a hybrid ink.

Although plenty of research has been done on the use of GO or rGO in Ag-graphene hybrid inks, to the best of our knowledge, there is no publications on the use of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in conductive inks. GQDs possess graphene properties such as excellent chemical and thermal stability and high surface area. They also offer unique properties such as high photoluminescence, biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity, which allows them to be used in a varioud fields of study such as solar cells, supercapacitors, and light-emitting diodes. [32–35]. However, their use in conductive inks has yet to be investigated.

Herein, three types of Ag-graphene hybrid conductive inks are prepared based on AgNPs, AgNWs, and Ag complex, and their preparation process, formulation, and morphology are studied. GQDs are first used to make Ag complex-graphene (AgGQDs) hybrid ink. An attempt is made to reduce the cost of ink by decreasing the amount of silver precursor and substituting it with rGO or GQDs while maintaining the electrical conductivity of the ink. By measuring the electrical conductivity of prepared films after the sintering process, the optimal values are obtained. The reasons for their differences are explained employing morphological studies. The effect of factors such as the mass ratio of silver nitrate and graphene oxide (AgNO3: GO), the change in the GO addition step to the process, and sintering temperature on the microstructure and electrical properties of prepared films is investigated in detail. Suitable printing methods for each type of ink, in the view of different applications are also introduced based on the ink and the printing method.

Silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.8%), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP-40, Mw ≈ 40,000 g/mol, and PVP-360, Mw ≈ 360,000 g/mol), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ethylene glycol (EG), citric acid monohydrate, and ethanolamine (99.0%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Graphene oxide (GO) aqueous dispersion and graphene oxide (GO) powder were received from Kara Pajuhesh Amirkabir Ltd. Silver carbonate (Ag2CO3, 99%) was purchased from Parsian Merck Novin Co. Ethylamine (C2H5NH2, 2.0 M in methanol), carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), and hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC, 2% in water-methanol solution) were acquired from Asia Pajuhesh Co. Formic acid, ammonium hydroxide, urea, glucose, ethanol, and acetone were obtained from Mojallali Co. Deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

The general route to prepare AgNPs/rGO nanocomposites was almost similar to that of AgNPs, as detailed in supplementary. Two methods were used for this purpose; In the first method, 10 ml of graphene oxide aqueous dispersion with a specific concentration was prepared. Then 15 ml of 0.24 M aqueous solution of PVP-40, and after that, 10 ml of 0.15 M aqueous solution of AgNO3 were added to the previous solution and heated up to 80 °C in an oil bath. After stirring for 10 min at 80 °C to ensure well blending of Ag+ and GO, the mixture was kept at this temperature without agitation for 2 h. Subsequently, 30 ml of 0.15 M aqueous solution of glucose and 1 M of NaOH were prepared and added dropwise to the previous solution. Glucose was added as a second reducing agent of GO and AgNO3, and the reduction was enhanced by adding NaOH. After 10 min, the reaction was completed. The solution was centrifuged twice at 8000 rpm for 5 min with distilled water and ethanol for removal of ions remained in solution and separation of nanocomposites. In the second method, the GO addition step was changed. GO aqueous dispersion was added after AgNO3 aqueous solution, and the remaining steps were completed in order. The AgNPs/rGO nanocomposites were named Ag/rGO1–20, Ag/rGO1–10, Ag/rGO1–8 (prepared by the first method), Ag/rGO2–20, Ag/rGO2–10, and Ag/rGO2–8 (prepared by the second method) corresponding to the mass ratio of silver nitrate to graphene oxide of 20:1, 10:1, and 8:1. GO concentration for preparation of Ag/rGO-20, Ag/rGO-10, and Ag/rGO-8 nanocomposites were 1.25, 2.5, and 3.125 g/L, respectively.

A solution of 0.07 M PVP-360 in ethylene glycol was prepared at room temperature, and then 0.25 g of AgNO3 was added in a typical polyol synthesis. After stirring for a few minutes, 2.5 ml of FeCl3 solution as a co-nucleation agent (600 µM in EG) was rapidly added to the previous solution. Fe3+ ions accelerate the rate of the polyol reduction, and the addition of Cl– ions reduces the number of free Ag+ during the formation of initial seed crystals and avoids the quick Ag+ supersaturation. The prepared solution was stirred for 2 min at room temperature and then transferred to a previously placed flask in an oil bath at 130 °C and soaked for 5 h. Before the end of the reaction, in the fourth hour, 10 ml of 2.5 mg/ml prepared GO solution in ethylene glycol was added to the reaction system. Synthesis of AgNWs/rGO nanocomposites was performed with the mass ratio of AgNO3: GO of 10, and named AgNWs/rGO-10. At the end, the nanowires were centrifuged twice at 4000 rpm for 5 min with ethanol and acetone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of AgNPs, AgNPs/rGO and AgNWs/rGO inks
ce:section.ce:para: First, a mixture of ethanol, deionized water, and ethylene glycol were prepared with volume ratios of 45:45:10, respectively. The reason for selecting these solvents is given in the supplementary. The prepared nanoparticles and nanocomposites were dispersed into the above solution under ultrasonication. The agglomerated particles and large undispersed aggregates were then separated by centrifuging at 2000 rpm for 2 min, resulting in water-based inks containing 5 wt% of solid content.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Ag and AgGQDs inks
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the electrical performance of the AgGQDs inks, firstly, an Ag-organic complex ink (Ag ink), then inks with different percentages of GQDs were prepared, as reported in Table 1.

To prepare AgGQDs inks, graphene quantum dots were prepared according to the method described in supplementary. A 1.8 M silver carbonate solution was then prepared in a mixture of ethylamine and ammonium hydroxide (60:40%vol.) to form an organic silver complex as a silver precursor. Thereafter, the desired values of GQDs were added to the previously prepared solution. 0.01 ml of formic acid as a reducing agent was added dropwise to the solution. Then 0.01 ml of ethanolamine was added for better dispersion of the reaction agents, and HEC 2% was mixed to adjust the ink viscosity. The prepared ink was kept at room temperature for 12 h. Finally, it was filtered using a 0.22 µm filter syringe and stored in the refrigerator.

The prepared conductive inks were deposited on the microscope slide coverslip glasses by a drop-coating method (in Fig. A.2 is shown) and sintered in an oven at selected temperatures for up to 15 min.

The crystalline structure of the samples was studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using Cu Kα and λ = 0.1542 nm. Ultraviolet-Visible spectra were obtained on an EV300 UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV–vis, Lambda 850, Perkin Elmer). Raman spectrum was obtained on a Takram P50C0R10 Dispersive Raman Microscope with 532 nm wavelength incident laser light. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of the AgNPs and Ag-graphene powder was performed using a Simultaneous Thermogravimetric Analyzer (STA 504, Bahr) in the air with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. The formation and surface morphology of the synthesized nanocomposites and printed patterns were investigated by Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM, MIRA3, TESCAN) equipped with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). The viscosity of the prepared inks was measured using a Rheometric Mechanical Spectrometer (RMS, MCR302, Anton Paar) at 25 °C. Inks surface tension was characterized using a fully automatic surface tension meter (K14, Kruss). The sheet resistance of the samples was measured by a four-probe method. The thickness of sintered films was measured using a Surface Profilometer to calculate the equivalent bulk resistivity.

To investigate the electrical conductivity of the prepared films, the sheet resistance was measured for at least three times and the mean values were recorded. The resistivity of the films was determined by measuring the film thickness and using Eq. (1):
(1) ρ = RS × t
where ρ, RS, and t are resistivity, average sheet resistance, and average thickness of the films, respectively [36]. The sintered films thickness can be set up by controlling the drop numbers of drop-coating. It was measured using a Surface Profilometer to be ∼ 3 µm on average. The thickness profile of one of the prepared films is shown in Fig. A.1.

Various methods are used to activate conductive inks having advantages and disadvantages. Herein to obtain conductive structures, activation of patterns was performed by thermal sintering method, and its effect on electrical resistivity was studied. According to reports, temperature and time of thermal activation are critical [37].

The electrical resistivity of AgNPs and Ag/rGO films after heat treatment for 15 min as a function of temperature is presented in Fig. 1. As shown in Fig. 1a, in the AgNPs ink, with increasing sintering temperature up to 250 °C, the resistivity decreases rapidly and reaches 1.97 × 10−5 Ω.cm. After that, with a further increase of sintering temperature, the resistivity gradually increases.

According to Fig. 1(b, c), in Ag/rGO nanocomposite films, with increasing sintering temperature from 150 °C to 400 °C, the resistivity rapidly decreases several times. In both methods, Ag/rGO-10 films show lower resistivity as temperature increases. With further temperature rise, the resistivity of Ag/rGO-20 films suddenly drops more dramatically than that of other nanocomposites. On the other hand, it was found that, unlike AgNPs ink, resistivity values continue to decrease with increasing temperature, indicating the superiority of Ag/rGO nanocomposites over AgNPs at higher temperatures.

For a more detailed study, Ag/rGO nanocomposites resistivity results with the same ratio of both preparation methods were compared with each other in Fig. 1(d-f). Interestingly, the decrease in resistivity in all formulations follows a similar pattern. At temperatures of 150–250 °C, Ag/rGO1 films show much lower resistivity than Ag/rGO2 films, and then at 250–400 °C, the decrease in resistivity of Ag/rGO2 films occurs more severely than Ag/rGO1 films. Also, an increase in resistivity is observed in Ag/rGO-20 films at 400 °C.

Table 2 shows the lowest electrical resistivity and the highest electrical conductivity of each sample at a given temperature. According to the indicated values, electrical conductivity of Ag/rGO nanocomposites inks is not comparable to AgNPs ink at low temperatures. On the contrary, at high temperatures, they have better electrical performance. The point of interest in the synthesis of AgNPs and Ag/rGO nanocomposites is the yield. Therefore, the yield was calculated by comparing the final mass of AgNPs and Ag/rGO nanocomposites with the theoretical amount of a complete reaction and was obtained about 40% and 70%, respectively. Therefore, it can reduce the final cost of Ag/rGO inks. This information is beneficial because a high conductive ink is not required in some applications, and Ag/rGO inks provide significant cost savings with moderate conductivity.

As shown in both methods, Ag/rGO-10 inks have the lowest electrical resistivity. In Fig. 2a, the electrical resistivity of AgNWs/rGO-10 film was measured to investigate the effect of Ag morphology on the electrical conductivity of prepared hybrid inks. It can be seen that as the sintering temperature increases to 300 °C, the electrical resistivity decreases. The optimal electrical conductivity of 5.56 × 103 S/cm (ρ = 1.80 × 10−4 Ω.cm) is obtained at 300 °C and above this temperature, AgNWs/rGO-10 electrical resistivity increases.

AgGQDs hybrid ink was investigated in terms of the percentage of the silver precursor replacement. To evaluate the electrical performance of AgGQDs ink, firstly electrical resistivity of Ag-organic complex ink (Ag ink) was measured. According to Bhat et al., the activation temperature and time of Ag ink were 100 °C for 30 min [15]. The effect of temperature on the electrical conductivity of Ag ink was re-examined. Fig. 2b illustrates how the electrical resistivity of Ag ink changes as the temperature rises. At 60 °C, the electrical resistivity is 8.4 × 10−4 Ω.cm and at 100 °C, 1.2 × 10−5 Ω.cm. Since the lowest electrical resistivity was obtained at a temperature of 100 °C, the other Ag-organic complex inks were subjected to heat treatment at this temperature. The electrical resistivity of films prepared with AgGQDs inks at 100 °C for 30 min was evaluated to investigate the effect of GQDs on electrical conductivity. Fig. 2c depicts the results in which the electrical resistivity increases up to 9 × 10−4 Ω.cm when the initial silver carbonate is reduced. The addition of GQDs to the ink improves electrical conductivity and significantly reduces resistivity. However, with a further increase of GQDs, electrical resistivity increases. The highest electrical conductivity of 5.29 × 103 S/cm (ρ = 1.89 × 10−4 Ω.cm) in AgGQDs2 film was achieved.

Formation of AgNPs and AgNPs/rGO nanocomposites and the attachment of the AgNPs onto graphene nanosheets, were confirmed using XRD, UV–vis, Raman, and TGA/DTA (See supplementary for more details). The reduction mechanism is the same for both methods of AgNPs/rGO nanocomposites synthesis. The oxygen-containing groups at the GO surface provide reactive sites and play a pivotal role in the nucleation and growth of AgNPs. Moreover, reduction of GO to rGO is performed by depositing AgNPs onto GO sheets in the presence of PVP under an alkaline condition and AgNPs act as nanoscale spacers between graphene sheets. Furthermore, glucose enhances the reduction intensity of GO and AgNO3 [11,38]. It is worth noting that GO partially reduces to rGO under the reaction condition and the rate of reduction definitely affects electrical conductivity. Therefore, it is not essential to be investigated independently.

The results of XRD and UV–vis studies indicate that in both methods of Ag/rGO nanocomposites preparation, high purity AgNPs are formed in the presence of GO. It can be observed that XRD patterns of pristine AgNPs and AgNPs/rGO hybrids have a close similarity and matching, as shown in Fig. 3a. The peaks of AgNPs are observed in all of the nanocomposites, which indicates that silver ions have been transformed into silver crystals [1]. The GO characteristic peak is not present in the AgNPs/rGO nanocomposites, resulting from the low percentage and the low diffraction intensity of rGO compared to the highly crystalline structure and vigorous diffraction intensity of AgNPs [39,40].

The charge transfer between GO and AgNPs and the reduction of GO during the process were investigated using Raman analysis. As shown in Fig. 3b, in the Raman spectra of Ag/rGO1–10 and Ag/rGO2–10, two peaks are observed at 641 cm−1 and approximately 1100 cm−1, which confirm the presence of silver in the nanocomposites. Furthermore, due to the surface enhanced Raman scattering effect originating from AgNPs, the intensity of both D and G bands has increased. This observation shows that the Raman enhancement is related to the effect of charge transfer between AgNPs and GO. The distance between the D and G bands in both nanocomposites has reduced by about 13 cm−1, indicating that GO has been reduced to rGO [41]. The intensity ratio of D and G bands in GO, Ag/rGO2–10 and Ag/rGO1–10 is 0.887, 0.788, and 0.665, respectively. Decreasing the ID/IG ratio suggests the restoration of sp2 domains and reduces the density of defects by removing functional groups during the GO reduction process [42,43]. The ID/IG ratio in Ag/rGO2–10 is higher than Ag/rGO1–10, and it can be concluded that in the first method, GO is highly reduced.

According to the TGA/DTA curves of the AgNPs sample in Fig. A.8, an exothermic peak and a weight loss at approximately 250 °C occurs, which attributes to the thermal degradation of PVP stabilizing molecules. That is why the electrical resistivity of AgNPs ink decreases rapidly in this temperature, which can also be seen in Fig. 1a [12]. In Ag/rGO1–10 nanocomposite, this weight loss of PVP desorption occurs in the temperature range of 180–350 °C since this temperature is highly dependent on the size of the nanoparticles. The larger the size, the higher the temperatures required for PVP degradation and sintering of the nanoparticles [44]. Therefore, FESEM images were used to investigate the effect of process and the mass ratio of AgNO3: GO on the size of nanoparticles and, consequently, the degradation temperature of nonconductive molecules influencing the ink final electrical conductivity.

Fig. 4, A.9, and A.10 show the typical FESEM images and size distribution diagrams of Ag/rGO nanocomposites. The simultaneous presence of AgNPs on the surface of GO nanosheets in these figures is relatively straightforward, indicating a strong interaction between GO and AgNPs. GO nanosheets with a significant surface area act as the carrier of AgNPs, and also, on the other side, AgNPs prevent the aggregation of rGO sheets [11]. There is a significant difference in the size and size distribution of AgNPs on graphene sheets, which is affected by the mass ratio of AgNO3: GO in the initial reaction. As the mass ratio increases, first, the number of AgNPs increases, and their size decreases. If the mass ratio exceeds a specific value, aggregation between particles occurs, and thus the particle size increases and the number decreases [26]. Considering the average size of nanoparticles and their standard deviation as a measure of nanoparticle distribution, in both methods, in samples with an initial ratio of AgNO3: GO = 10, nanoparticles with the smallest size and the minimum size distribution are evenly dispersed on the surface of rGO nanosheets. Consequently, it can be said that Ag/rGO1–10 and Ag/rGO2–10 nanocomposites have the best morphology, and their average nanoparticle size is almost the same, 29 nm and 28 nm, respectively. AgNPs accumulate, and more aggregations form as the mass ratio increases, as observed in Fig. 4(c and f). As a result, the number of particles decreases, their size increases, and it results into the size distribution non-uniformity. Although large-sized nanoparticles are found in other samples, they are more frequent in Ag/rGO-20 nanocomposites, and AgNPs dispersion on rGO sheets is less than in other samples. Besides, Fig. 4a clearly shows a transparent, wrinkled, and ultra-thin layer on the nanoparticles, which can be related to GO sheets and may indicate no reduction of GO in the synthesis process. Wrinkled GO sheets are observed in Fig. 4d. It appears, with increasing the amount of AgNO3, the number of wrinkles decreases, and the reduction process of GO is better accomplished [45,46].

Now some of the reasons for observed trends in the study of electrical conductivity of Ag/rGO inks in Section 3.1., can be explained here. Silver nanoparticles must first contact each other by removing the stabilizer to form conductive paths. According to Fig. 1a, if the nanoparticles in nanocomposites were at the same size as AgNPs, complete removal of PVP would have occurred at 250 °C. Therefore, reducing the resistivity to about 300 °C is related to the stabilizer removal, which is consistent with TGA results. In both methods, Ag/rGO-10 films show the lowest resistivity up to this temperature. As indicated by FESEM, the smaller the size of silver nanoparticles in the ratio of AgNO3: GO = 10, the better the sintering process and lower the resistivity in this ratio. On the other hand, as explained in Raman results, the first method is more effective at reducing GO. Therefore, Ag/rGO1 inks are expected to have higher electrical conductivity as observed at 150–250 °C, for Ag/rGO1 and Ag/rGO2 films.

The AgNWs formation in the presence of GO was confirmed using the XRD analysis of AgNWs and AgNWs/rGO powders, Fig. 5a. Four peaks at about 38.21°, 44.39°, 65.54°, and 77.49°, representing the (111), (200), (220) and (311) planes of silver, respectively. SEM images of AgNWs/rGO-10 composite film at different magnifications imply that the high purity AgNWs with the aspect ratio of about 50 were successfully synthesized using the polyol method, Fig. 5b. In this method, higher temperature and longer time could enhance the PVP and EG reduction effect. Under this condition, GO can be selectively reduced by EG and epoxy groups are mainly reduced [47]. On the other hand, nanowires could be placed between nanosheets before reaction completion and prevent decreasing the distance between them. AgNWs are also entirely distributed on GO layers, indicating a strong interaction between GO and AgNWs. Complete coverage of graphene sheets with Ag nanowires is well defined. No other silver nanostructures, such as spheres, are observed, so the synthesis has been successful. Low resistivity results from forming a three-dimensional conductive network in AgNWs/rGO-10 nanocomposite, which increases the number of conductive paths.

The structural study of AgGQDs inks was also performed by examining the morphology of the prepared films from these inks and the difference in electrical resistivity (Fig. 2) was explained. supplementary provides more information on AgGQDs ink, including XRD and EDS analysis. According to the schematic illustration and FESEM images in Fig. 6, reducing silver carbonate leads to a decrease in the final silver loading and creates pores. As a result, their connection and aggregation decrease, and the resistivity increases, Fig. 6(a–c). The addition of GQDs to the ink improves electrical conductivity and significantly reduces resistivity. The presence of GQDs as a conductive agent may have led to the establishment of new bridges between silver particles and enable electrical charge transportation, Fig. 6(a, d). Nevertheless, with the further increase of GQDs, perhaps due to the more significant accumulation of GQDs and the prevention of solvent evaporation, less conductive silver pathways are formed, and the electrical resistivity increases, Fig. 6a.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of sintering temperature on electrical conductivity
ce:section.ce:para: FESEM images were used to investigate the effect of sintering temperature on the electrical conductivity of conductive films. Fig. 7 shows the FESEM images of AgNPs films at three different temperatures. As shown in Fig. 7(a, b), sintering at lower temperatures has negligible effects on the morphology of AgNPs due to the protective effect of PVP molecules. As the sintering temperature increases, PVP molecules begin to be removed from the surface of AgNPs and bind to each other to form a neck, creating conductive pathways and improving electrical conductivity. Some of these paths are shown in Fig. 6d [13].

On the other hand, several nanoparticles fusion and assembly form large clusters during the sintering process and lead to grain growth [48]. As the sintering temperature increases to 350 °C, the grain growth and number increase, and the shape of the grains become completely spherical, Fig. 7f. Grain formation leads to discontinuous island structures, making electron transfer impossible, thus sheet resistance and resistivity increase at temperatures above 250 °C. Constructing an interconnected metal network is necessary to achieve electrical conductivity in the material [37]. Some of network discontinuities are shown in Fig. 7f.

FESEM images in Fig. 8 show the microstructure of Ag/rGO2–8 and Ag/rGO2–20 films after sintering for 15 min at 200 °C and 400 °C. Significant differences can be seen in the morphology of Ag/rGO films. Carefully in the images, two types of morphology can be considered in all of them; Graphene nanosheets densely coated with agglomerated silver nanoparticles[1] and graphene nanosheets, including a smoother surface with fewer silver nanoparticles. For example, in Fig. 8a, some of these nanosheets are specified. In Ag/rGO2–8 sample, AgNPs are smaller at 200 °C, and on the other hand, there are many holes in the film, and it is not uniform, Fig. 8a. As the temperature rises to 400 °C, more particles come into contact with each other, grow through the neck formation, and the larger nanoparticles form a dense structure [5]. It should be noted that the pores between the nanoparticles are fewer and smaller at high temperatures, Fig. 8b.

Interestingly, graphene sheets in this nanocomposite prevent the emergence of grains and an island structure formation. Because of the strong interaction of AgNPs and graphene sheets that act as supporters, the nanoparticles cannot separate from the graphene surface. Therefore, according to Fig. 1, the superiority of using Ag/rGO inks compared to AgNPs ink at high temperatures was determined.

In the study of the electrical conductivity of the Ag/rGO inks in Fig. 1(b, c), it was mentioned that in the first method at 350 °C and the second method at 300 °C, the resistivity of Ag/rGO-20 films suddenly showed a more severe drop compared to other nanocomposites. It can be argued that at this temperature, all AgNPs are well sintered and connected. Because the amount of AgNPs in this ratio is higher than others, the resistivity is further reduced. In the Ag/rGO2–20 sample, at 400 °C, as shown in Fig. 8d, the lattice structure is destroyed, and large cavities appear. This is probably due to the nanoparticles non-uniform distribution on graphene nanosheets in the primary nanocomposite (shown in Fig. 4). Some nanosheets are not covered with nanoparticles, and the oxidation of graphene nanosheets in the air caused their destruction [27]. It may also be the phenomenon of grain emergence and growth due to the low percentage of rGO in its composition. These could be possible reasons for the increase in electrical resistivity observed in Ag/rGO-20 films at 400 °C in Fig. 1f.

Fig. 9 shows SEM images of nanocomposite films of AgNWs/rGO-10 after sintering at different temperatures. Fig. 9(a, b), shows formation of AgNWs random networks on the graphene surface and the connection between the nanowires [49]. Since PVP is not completely degraded and its protective effect remains, as the temperature rises from 100 ℃ to 170 ℃, minor morphological changes happen to create a slightly smoother surface. As the sintering temperature increases, a more significant accumulation of AgNWs is observed, insofar as at 300 °C, according to Fig. 9c. Complete degradation of PVP and sintering of AgNWs leads to better contact between the nanowires and the creation of conductive bridges and new electron transport pathways. This explains why higher sintering temperature yields better electrical conductivity, as shown in Fig. 2a. The smooth and even surface is also visible. However, in some areas, structural defects of AgNWs are also observed, Fig. 9c. Fig. 9d shows that the surface morphology has completely changed, and due to the high sintering temperature, oxidation and degradation of AgNWs and graphene occurs, and a porous surface island is formed. Increase in electrical resistivity of AgNWs/rGO-10 in Fig. 2a, may be attributed to the oxidation and degradation of AgNWs and graphene.

Ag-organic complex ink (Ag ink) activated at different temperatures to investigate the effect of temperature on conductive patterns morphology and electrical performance. Fig. 10 shows the FESEM images of the Ag films at 60 °C and 100 °C for 30 min. As can be seen, with increasing temperature at 100 °C, the structure is completely changed, the silver nanoparticles are grown, are densely bonded together, and no space is observed between them. Thus FESEM observations confirm the decreasing electrical resistivity trend with increasing temperature, as described in Fig. 2b.

In this section, first, the printability of prepared hybrid inks for inkjet printing was investigated. Considering the fact that the AgNWs/rGO nanocomposites can be widely used for screen printing due to their morphology, an attempt was made to provide a suitable formulation for AgNWs/rGO-10 ink. The viscosity and surface tension of the 5 wt% Ag/rGO1–10 and AgGQDs1 ink are requied to be studied and adjusted to ensure proper jetting of the inks through the inkjet printer nozzle. The measured viscosity at different shear rates at 25 °C is shown in Fig. 11(a, b). The graphs reveal that as the shear rate increases, the viscosity decreases, indicating the behavior of the shear-thinning pseudoplastic of the inks. Also, little change in ink viscosity is observed from low to high shear rates, making it suitable for a wide range of printing methods.

Other measured physical properties of Ag/rGO1–10 and AgGQDs1 inks are shown in Table A.2. Also, the Dimatix Materials Printer DMP-2800 inkjet printing conditions are listed in Table A.3. Based on this information, the nondimensional numbers Re, We, and Z are calculated (as described in supplementary). According to the obtained numbers, it is determined that the inks are in the proper printable window. On the other hand, since the Z numbers are 2 < Z < 10, it is possible to form stable droplets [50].

The viscosity required for screen printing should be in the range of 50–5000 mPa.s. Equal amounts of carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) and hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC) were used as viscosity modifiers to increase the viscosity of AgNWs/rGO-10 ink. The viscosity at different shear rates for each of these two inks in Fig. 11c clearly shows that both inks have a thixotropic behavior. The ink viscosity decreases with increasing shear rate [51]. However, as it turns out, the viscosity of the ink prepared with CMC is several times lower than that of formulated with HEC, and therefore it could be accounted more appropriate.

Several conductive hybrid inks were developed and classified based on the preparation process, formulation, and morphology. The electrical conductivity of prepared inks was studied and the reasons were investigated using morphological and structural studies. It was found that in AgNPs/rGO inks, the strong interaction of AgNPs and graphene sheets that act as supporters prevented the formation of an island structure in films at high temperatures. Despite the fact that electrical conductivity of Ag/rGO inks is less than that of AgNPs inks, they can provide required conductivity for some applications. AgNWs in AgNWs/rGO ink achieved high electrical conductivity at low temperatures by creating three-dimensional networks and electron transfer paths. GQDs in AgGQDs ink improved electrical conductivity and compensated for reducing the silver precursor. Moreover, thermal sintering at different temperatures played an essential role in the printed films electrical conductivity. Increasing the sintering temperature reduced the electrical resistivity by evaporating the solvent, degrading nonconductive molecules, and connecting conductive agents. At very high temeperature, however; the degradation or oxidation of the particles caused an increase in electrical resistivity. The highest conductivity of prepared inks ranged from 2.67 × 102 S/cm to 4.0 × 104 S/cm. Finally, the most suitable printing method and ink printability were also suggested.

Saeedeh Ghadimi: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Project administration, made detail analyses of the collected data, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Ali Mohammad Bazargan: Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Data curation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation. Saeedeh Mazinani and Farhad Sharif: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Room-temperature synthesis and enhanced catalytic performance of silver-reduced graphene oxide nanohybrids

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.V. Thu, P.J. Ko, N.H.H. Phuc, A. Sandhu, Room-temperature synthesis and enhanced catalytic performance of silver-reduced graphene oxide nanohybrids, J. Nanoparticle Res. 15 (2013) 1–13. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11051‐013‐1975‐9.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silver Nanoparticles as Low-emissivity Coating Materials for Window Glazing Applications, in: NSTI Adv. Mater. - TechConnect Briefs

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Wadhwa, D. Kumar, S. Mahendia, S. Kumar, Microwave assisted facile synthesis of reduced graphene oxide-silver (RGO-Ag) nanocomposite and their application as active SERS substrate, Mater. Chem. Phys. 194 (2017) 274–282. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2017.03.045.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of silver nanoparticles-graphene oxide nanocomposite and its application in electrochemical sensing of tryptophan

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Li, D. Kuang, Y. Feng, F. Zhang, Z. Xu, M. Liu, D. Wang, Green synthesis of silver nanoparticles-graphene oxide nanocomposite and its application in electrochemical sensing of tryptophan, Biosens. Bioelectron. 42 (2013) 198–206. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2012.10.029.
C. Xu, R.S. Yuan, X. Wang, Selective reduction of graphene oxide, Xinxing Tan Cailiao/New Carbon Mater. 29 (2014) 61–66. https://doi.org/10.1016/S1872‐5805(14)60126‐8.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Conductive network structure formed by laser sintering of silver nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Qin, A. Watanabe, Conductive network structure formed by laser sintering of silver nanoparticles, J. Nanoparticle Res. 16 (2014) 1–12. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11051‐014‐2684‐8.
